sections.innerHtml:
The Deliverables pipeline panel (#deliv) is the dashboard's program-management lens: for the selected study it shows how many deliverables are done versus total in each of four lifecycle phases. It is a scaled echo of the Protocol-to-CSR deliverables register, so reading it well means reading the shape of the whole study lifecycle, not just a single bar. This section also covers the two portfolio-level readouts that aggregate deliverables across all eight studies: the Avg deliverables complete KPI and the Portfolio trend, plus how to find the crunch points.

What the panel shows

The card is full-width and holds one bar per phase, in lifecycle order. The four phase keys (s.deliv) are fixed:

  • Startup — Protocol, SAP, CRF/eCRF design, randomization spec, analysis-dataset specs and the early planning deliverables.
  • Conduct — deliverables produced while the study is enrolling and running: data-management plan execution, interim/DSMB outputs, ongoing programming specs.
  • Analysis — the analysis-stage deliverables: ADaM datasets, the bulk of the TLF program, derivations and define documentation.
  • Reporting — the closeout deliverables: final TLFs, statistical report inputs and CSR contributions.

For each phase the bar is rendered as follows:

bar width = pct(done / total)  ·  bar color = ragColor((1 − done/total) × 90)  ·  label = "done/total"

Note the color polarity: the RAG color is driven by the incomplete fraction. A phase that is mostly finished (high done/total) yields a small (1 − ratio)×90 value and reads green; a phase that is barely started reads red. The ×90 factor means a phase has to be almost untouched (roughly under 27% complete) before it tips red, and below about 63% complete before it goes amber — phases that are well underway stay green.

How to read it phase-by-phase

The synthetic generator deliberately lags later phases behind earlier ones: each phase's completion is pulled down by its position in the lifecycle (an increasing per-phase lag term plus a stage-weight divisor and small noise), so Startup tracks closest to the study's overall progress while Reporting trails furthest. The expected, healthy picture for a study still in its early-to-middle life is therefore a left-to-right downward staircase: Startup fullest, Reporting emptiest. That is normal and not by itself a problem.

Read the panel relative to the study's phase, not in absolute terms. A Reporting-phase study with an empty Reporting bar is a real crunch point; a Conduct-phase study with an empty Reporting bar is simply work that has not started yet. Always pair this panel with the milestone track (#ms) and the study's phase shown in #msTitle to decide whether a low bar is a concern.

Per-phase deliverable counts (register scale)

The four phases are sized from a fixed register so the demo mirrors the 136-item Protocol→CSR register at scale. Totals per phase are fixed:

PhaseRegister total (per study)Stage weightTypical completeness as study matures
Startup141.00Fills first; usually green by Conduct
Conduct260.85Climbs through the conduct phase
Analysis340.55Largest bucket; green only near/after DB lock
Reporting200.20Fills last; expected red until Reporting phase

The done count for each phase is min(total, round(total × clamp₀₁(prog / weight − index×0.15 + noise))), where prog is the study's overall lifecycle progress (0..1), index is the phase position (Startup 0 → Reporting 3), clamp₀₁ bounds the completion fraction to [0, 1], and noise = rf(−0.05, 0.05). The combination of the divisor and the increasing index×0.15 lag term is what pushes later phases down relative to prog. Because Analysis is the largest bucket (34) and sits mid-lifecycle, it is usually the phase that signals the analysis-readiness squeeze before DB lock — watch it climb as prog rises.

Reading overall portfolio progress

Two readouts roll deliverables up to the whole portfolio.

Avg deliverables complete (KPI strip, #kpis) is the headline number:

Avg deliverables complete = mean over the 8 studies of (Σ done / Σ total) × 100, rounded  ·  green if >70, amber if >45, else red

This averages each study's overall deliverable completion (summing done and total across all four phases first, then taking the ratio), then averages those eight study-level percentages. Because it pools all phases, a portfolio dominated by early-phase studies will read lower even when every individual study is on track — interpret it together with the phase mix in the roster, not as a standalone grade.

Portfolio trend (#trend) plots the cumulative TLFs finalized line (blue, #4ea8ff) alongside the open-queries line (amber, #e6b450) across 8 weeks. The deliverables register itself is not trended week-by-week; the finalized-TLF line is the closest proxy for reporting-deliverable velocity. The legend shows current values q[7] and f[7] (newest week).

The TLF line in the portfolio trend is a synthetic ramp, not a stored historical series — f[w] = Σ round(finalized × (0.35 + 0.65·w/7)) across the 8 weeks. It illustrates direction of travel for the demo only. With real data wired in (see go-live), this would be replaced by the actual finalized-TLF time series from the TLF program tracker. Do not read week-over-week TLF deltas as a measured rate in the demo.

Finding the crunch points

A crunch point is a study whose remaining deliverable load is concentrated in a phase that is due now. Use this workflow:

1. Scan the roster (#tbl), sorted by Risk descending (default). The riskiest studies float to the top.
2. For each high-risk study, check its phase column. A study in Reporting or DB Lock is where deliverable lag matters most.
3. Open the study (click the row or a heatmap cell) and read the Deliverables pipeline bars. A red Reporting or Analysis bar on a late-phase study is the crunch point — the work that must close before CSR.
4. Cross-check the milestone track (#ms) for any risk (red) milestone dots — TLFs and CSR milestones flag overdue reporting deliverables directly.
5. Confirm against the early-warning signals (#signals): a TLF production behind signal (fires when phase is Reporting or DB Lock and finalized/planned < 0.6) corroborates an empty Reporting bar near the lock/report date.
In the seeded demo, CP-108 is deliberately forced into an at-risk Reporting state (phase Reporting, DB lock 24 days past, TLFs and CSR milestones set to risk). It is the canonical crunch-point example: late phase, but its reporting-side deliverables and TLF funnel are far behind. Use it to learn the read; it is not a real study.

How this ties to scoring

The deliverables panel is descriptive — it is not a direct input to the four risk dimensions. The TLF and Resource dimensions are driven by the TLF program tracker and programming metadata (finalized/planned, double-programmed %, open discrepancies), not by the register counts. So the deliverables bars give you the program-management context behind a study's risk score without double-counting it. When a study scores red on Timeline or TLF, the deliverables pipeline tells you which lifecycle phase is carrying the unfinished work.

Where the numbers come from

In the demo every deliverable count is SIMULATED, generated deterministically from the seed rng(20260621) and reproducible run to run. At go-live the panel is fed read-only by the Protocol→CSR deliverables register via deliverablesRegister(s.id), returning {phase: {done, total}} for Startup / Conduct / Analysis / Reporting; only operational metadata crosses — no participant-level data.

This dashboard is operations-only decision-support and is not validated software. It carries no PHI, no participant-level clinical data, and no reported clinical numbers. Any reported or regulated value — finalized PK parameters, statistical outputs, dataset conformance — comes only from validated tools (Phoenix WinNonlin, Pinnacle 21, the EDC/CTMS and the TLF program tracker), never from this panel. Deliverable counts here track workflow status, not deliverable content. Humans decide; the dashboard only surfaces where to look.

For participant-safety signals — clinical and laboratory monitoring at the participant level — use the companion TRIALMON_Dashboard.html. This Study Lifecycle Monitor (Study_Lifecycle_Monitor.html) is the operational/program complement: it watches deliverables, data readiness, TLF production and milestones, not participant-level clinical signals.

sections.innerHtml:
The Study roster (#tbl) and the per-study detail modal (#modal / #modalBox) are the bottom of the funnel: the heatmap and signal panels tell you which study and which dimension deserves attention, the roster lets you triage all eight studies in one sortable table, and the modal gives you the full per-study picture before you act. This section covers how to sort and triage the roster, exactly what the modal shows, and the recommended drill-down path from a colored cell or fired signal to a defensible operational decision.

The roster table: columns and what each one tells you

The roster lists all eight studies (CP-101 through CP-108) with nine columns. It opens sorted by overall risk descending (sortKey='overall', sortDir=-1), so the most at-risk study sits at the top on load — in the demo that is the deliberately forced at-risk study CP-108.

ColumnFieldHow to read it
Studys.idThe study identifier (CP-101…CP-108). Click anywhere in the row to open the modal.
Phases.phaseOne of Startup, Conduct, DB Lock, Reporting. Drives the reporting/DBL pressure weights in scoring.
Designs.designThe study design label.
Leads.ownerThe responsible owner — your first call when a row is red.
Enrolledenrolled/targetRecruitment progress against target.
Open Qs.data.openQOpen EDC queries. Cell turns amber when openQ > 40.
Cleans.data.cleanPctPercent of data clean.
TLF finalfinalized/plannedFinalized TLFs against planned — the headline output-production ratio.
Risks.sc.overallRAG swatch (rag(overall)) plus the rounded overall score. The triage column.

Sorting and triage

Click any column header to sort by that column; clicking the same header again toggles the direction (if(sortKey===k) sortDir*=-1; else { sortKey=k; sortDir=-1; }). Use the columns as a triage lens rather than reading rows top-to-bottom:

  • Risk (default) — start here. Reds first, ordered worst-to-best, gives you the portfolio's escalation queue in one glance.
  • Open Q — sort descending to find the studies driving the portfolio query backlog; amber cells (>40) jump out.
  • TLF final — sort ascending to surface reporting-phase studies whose output production is lagging.
  • Phase — group Reporting and DB Lock studies together, since those are where TLF and double-programming pressure bite hardest.
  • Lead — group by owner when you are preparing a per-lead status conversation.
The Risk column and the heatmap share the same RAG thresholds: rag(v) returns 'g' (green, on track) when v ≤ 33, 'a' (amber, watch) when 33 < v ≤ 66, and 'r' (red, at risk) when v > 66.
A red swatch in the roster and a red cell in the heatmap mean exactly the same thing. But a study can carry a green overall while still hiding one red dimension — always confirm in the modal or heatmap before concluding "fine."

Opening the modal: entry points

The same modal opens from two places, and the entry point changes what the header tells you:

  • From a roster row — click any row. The modal opens on that study with no dimension focus.
  • From a heatmap cell — click a cell. This sets SEL to that study, re-renders the selected-study panels (milestones, data readiness, TLF funnel, deliverables), and opens the modal with a focus dimension noted in the header subline (e.g. focus: Data), telling you which column's color sent you there.

You can also set the selected study from the #studySel dropdown in the header bar, which re-renders all selected-study panels but does not open the modal.

What the modal shows for a single study

The modal (#modalBox) has three parts: a header, an eight-tile key-value grid, and a provenance note.

Header. id — name on the top line; a subline reading design · phase · Lead owner, with · focus: dim appended if you arrived from a heatmap cell.

Eight KV tiles — the full operational snapshot in one view:

TileShows
Overall riskRounded overall score plus its label (on track / watch / at risk).
Timeline / DataThe two dimension scores side by side.
TLF / ResourceThe other two dimension scores.
Enrollmentenrolled/target plus number of sites.
Open queries · clean · SDVEDC data-readiness trio in one tile.
Days to DB lockdaysToDBL (negative means the planned lock date has passed).
TLF cascadeplanned → programmed → qcd → finalized counts.
Double-programmed % · discrepanciesprog.dblProgPct and prog.discrep — the independent-QC programming status.

Provenance note ("Where these numbers would come from") is always open and maps each block to its real source: milestones → CTMS; enrollment, queries, clean, SDV → EDC; the TLF funnel → the TLF program tracker; deliverables → the Protocol→CSR register.

Close the modal with the × button or by clicking the backdrop.

Recommended drill-down workflow

1. Scan the heatmap or signals. A red heatmap cell or a red early-warning signal names the study and the problem dimension. This is your trigger.
2. Click the red cell (not just the row). This focuses SEL on the study, re-renders the selected-study panels, and opens the modal with the focus: dim note so you know which dimension drove the color.
3. Read the KV tiles to see whether one dimension is isolated or several are elevated. Cross-check the focus dimension's tile against its inputs — e.g. a red Data score against the Open queries · clean · SDV tile, or a red TLF score against the TLF cascade tile.
4. Confirm the operational drivers in the dedicated selected-study panels the cell-click just refreshed: the milestone track for overdue keys, data readiness for the query sparkline and days-to-DBL, and the TLF funnel for where the pipeline stalls (programmed vs. qcd vs. finalized).
5. Identify the owner from the modal subline or the roster Lead column, then act through your normal channel — query-resolution push, TLF QC reprioritization, double-programming catch-up, or a milestone escalation.
6. Use the roster to widen the lens. Re-sort by the same problem column (e.g. Open Q descending) to check whether the issue is isolated to one study or a portfolio-wide pattern needing a shared response.
Every number in the roster and modal is SIMULATED, seeded demo data — not real study data. The dashboard is operations-only: it carries no PHI, no participant-level data, and no reported clinical numbers. It is decision-support to help you triage and prioritize, not validated software and not a source of record. Any reported or regulated value — PK parameters, dataset conformance, or anything in a deliverable — comes only from validated tools (Phoenix WinNonlin, Pinnacle 21, the EDC/CTMS systems of record). A human always makes the call; treat a red cell as "go look," never as "the answer."
This Study Lifecycle Monitor watches the program — deliverables, data readiness, TLF production, and milestones. Participant-safety signals live in the companion TRIALMON_Dashboard.html. When a row here looks operationally fine but you need the clinical-safety view, that is the dashboard to open instead.

sections.innerHtml:
The Portfolio trend panel (#trend) is the only panel that shows direction of travel for the whole portfolio rather than a single snapshot. Where the KPI strip and heatmap tell you where you are right now, this panel tells you which way the portfolio is moving over the last 8 weeks. It plots two lines on a single SVG canvas: portfolio-wide open queries (amber) and cumulative TLFs finalized (blue). You read it to answer one question fast: is the operational load building or draining?

What the two lines are

  • Open queries (amber, #e6b450) — for each week w, q[w] is the sum across all 8 studies of that study's weekly query level (s.data.qtrend[w]). This is a real back-cast series: each study carries an 8-week query trend ending at its current open-query count (qtrend[7] = openQ), and the panel sums them week-by-week. Down is good — the portfolio is closing more queries than it opens.
  • TLFs finalized (blue, #4ea8ff) — f[w] is a cumulative count of finalized TLFs across studies. Up is good — output is being produced and signed off.

The TLF line is not a stored historical series. Per spec, f[w] = sum over studies of Math.round(s.tlf.finalized*(0.35+0.65*w/(W-1))) — a synthetic ramp anchored to each study's current finalized count and smeared backward over 8 weeks. It always rises monotonically by construction, so the TLF line's shape is illustrative, not measured. Trust its endpoint (f[7] = today's portfolio finalized total, shown in the legend as "TLFs finalized (cumulative): now N") and read its rise as "output exists," but do not interpret week-to-week TLF slope as a real production cadence. The open-query (amber) line is the honest direction signal; weight your reading toward it.

How to read direction and momentum

Two independent scales: each line is normalized to its own maximum within the canvas (viewBox 0 0 1000 150, pad 26). The lines are not on a shared y-axis, so do not compare their heights to each other — never read "the lines crossed" as meaningful. Read each line only against its own earlier points. Points are plotted left (oldest) to right (newest), one per week. The legend prints the two current values, q[7] and f[7].

Direction (amber, queries): compare q[7] (newest) to q[0] (oldest). Falling = backlog draining. Rising = backlog building.
Momentum (amber): compare the last 3 weeks, q[7] − q[4]. This is the exact quantity the "Query backlog rising" signal fires on (>10 amber, >25 red).
1. Glance at the amber line's overall slope across all 8 weeks — that is portfolio direction.
2. Now look only at the last three amber points (weeks 5→7). A steepening climb there is momentum — the situation is accelerating, not just elevated. The portfolio-level q[7] − q[4] echoes the per-study "Query backlog rising" trigger in the signals panel.
3. Confirm the blue line ends higher than it started (it should, by construction). If f[7] in the legend is low relative to total planned TLFs, output is thin regardless of slope — corroborate against the per-study TLF funnel (#funnel) and the "TLF production behind" signal.
4. Cross-check the endpoints against the KPI strip: q[7] should equal the "Open queries (portfolio)" KPI (both are sum of s.data.openQ). If your eye and the KPI disagree, trust the KPI number.

Reading the four shapes

Amber (queries)Blue (TLFs)What it meansWhat to do
FallingRisingHealthy portfolio: cleaning is winning and output is flowing.Routine review. Spot-check the at-risk study still trends right.
FlatRisingNet-zero queries: opened ≈ closed. Cleaning is treading water.Investigate which studies are stalling — flat at portfolio level can hide one study surging while another drains. Drill via the heatmap Data column.
RisingRisingWrong-direction queries: backlog building even as TLFs finalize. Often a Conduct-phase study ramping enrollment.Find the contributor(s). Check the "Query backlog rising" signal for the study id and the +N magnitude.
RisingFlat / low endpointWorst pattern near a lock: load building, output not moving.Escalate. Pair with "DB lock at risk" and "TLF production behind" signals; open the per-study modal.

Flat is not neutral. A flat amber line means new queries are arriving as fast as old ones close — steady-state churn, not resolution. In an early-phase portfolio you usually want the amber line trending down as studies move from Conduct toward DB Lock. A persistently flat portfolio line, or one that's flat only because a draining study masks a climbing one, is a prompt to leave the portfolio view and use the heatmap (#heat) Data column to find the per-study story.

What a wrong-direction or flat trend should prompt

This panel does not name the culprit study — it is portfolio-summed. When the amber line rises or refuses to fall, treat it as a pointer into the rest of the dashboard:

  1. Go to the signals panel (#signals). A rising portfolio query line almost always corresponds to one or more "Query backlog rising" signals, each tagged with a study id (who) and the net growth over the last 3 weeks. Red there means q[7]−q[4] > 25 for that study.
  2. Open the heatmap Data cells. Sort is by overall risk; scan the Data column for amber/red. Click a cell to open the per-study modal focused on Data.
  3. In the modal, read the open-query sparkline and days-to-DB-lock line. A backlog rising into a near DB lock is the "DB lock at risk" condition (daysToDBL>0 && <21 && openQ>15).
  4. If the blue line is flat/low near reporting, check "TLF production behind" and "Double-programming lag" signals and the per-study TLF funnel.

The open-query and finalized counts shown here are operational metadata only — counts of queries and document sign-offs, never PHI, never participant-level clinical data, never a reported clinical result. Any regulated or reported number comes only from validated tools (EDC/CTMS for queries and milestones, the TLF program tracker for finalized counts, and Phoenix WinNonlin / Pinnacle 21 for analysis outputs) — never from this trend line. The trend is decision-support to tell you where to look; a human decides what it means.

Demo-data caveat. In the shipped Study_Lifecycle_Monitor.html the series are SIMULATED and seeded (rng(20260621), reproducible). The forced at-risk study CP-108 contributes a deliberately ugly profile (finalized at ~10% of planned, DBL already past). At go-live the same lines are fed read-only from EDC (edcMetrics(s.id) supplies openQ and the real qtrend[8]) and the TLF program tracker (tlfTrackerCounts(s.id)) — at which point you can replace the synthetic TLF ramp with a true finalized-by-week series and read its slope as real cadence. This is not validated software; it complements TRIALMON_Dashboard.html, which watches participant-safety signals rather than operational throughput.

sections.innerHtml:
Out of the box, Study_Lifecycle_Monitor.html is fully self-contained: it generates all eight studies (CP-101 through CP-108) from a seeded pseudo-random generator, rng(20260621), so every reviewer sees the identical green/amber/red spread. Every number on the screen is SIMULATED DEMO DATA. This section explains the single seam where you swap that synthetic generator for your real operational systems — the DATA ADAPTER — and documents exactly which read-only source feeds each panel and field.

Illustrative until wired. Nothing on this dashboard is a reported, regulated, or submission-grade number, and it never will be. Reported/regulated values come only from validated tools (Phoenix WinNonlin, Pinnacle 21, your validated EDC/CTMS). This monitor is decision-support for triage and conversation; humans decide. It is not validated software.

The one seam: loadPortfolio()

The whole dashboard reads from one array, STUDIES. In demo mode that array is built by calling genStudy(i) eight times. The DATA ADAPTER comment block holds an async stub, loadPortfolio(), whose only job is to return that same array of study objects — same field names, same shape — but populated from your live systems instead of the generator. Go-live is a one-line swap:

STUDIES = await loadPortfolio(); // replaces: STUDIES = Array.from({length:8}, (_,i)=>genStudy(i))

Because every panel already reads the canonical study-object shape that genStudy returns, you do not touch any panel code. You only implement the four source readers and the merge function inside loadPortfolio().

Four read-only sources, one merge

The adapter pulls from four operational systems and merges their fields into the demo's field names. Each reader is keyed by study id so the dashboard stays organized per study:

  • ctmsStudies() — CTMS: returns id, phase, the seven milestones (Protocol..CSR with dates and status), enrolled/target, and sites.
  • edcMetrics(s.id) — EDC: returns openQ, qtrend[8], cleanPct, sdvPct, daysToDBL.
  • tlfTrackerCounts(s.id) — TLF program tracker: returns planned, programmed, qcd, finalized.
  • deliverablesRegister(s.id) — Protocol→CSR deliverables register: returns {phase:{done,total}} for Startup/Conduct/Analysis/Reporting.

A small merge helper, mapToModel(e, t, d), folds the EDC, TLF, and deliverables payloads onto the CTMS skeleton and produces the canonical study object the panels expect.

Go-live transport. Wire each source read-only — either a REST pull at page load or a nightly CSV export that loadPortfolio() parses. Read-only is the contract: the dashboard observes operational metadata and never writes back to CTMS, EDC, the tracker, or the register.

Which source feeds each panel

This is the authoritative mapping. Build your readers to deliver exactly these fields and every panel lights up correctly.

PanelSourceFields
Milestone track + Portfolio health (Timeline dimension)CTMSid, phase, milestones (Protocol..CSR with dates/status), enrolled/target, sites
Data readiness + Data dimensionEDCopenQ, qtrend[8], cleanPct, sdvPct, daysToDBL
TLF production funnel + TLF dimensionTLF program trackerplanned, programmed, qcd, finalized
Deliverables pipelineProtocol→CSR deliverables register{phase:{done,total}} for Startup / Conduct / Analysis / Reporting
Resource dimensionTLF tracker / programming metadataprog.dblProgPct (double-programmed %), prog.discrep (open discrepancies)
Provenance in the product. This same mapping is surfaced to users inside the per-study modal's "Where these numbers would come from" note: Milestones→CTMS · enrollment/queries/clean/SDV→EDC · TLF funnel→TLF program tracker · deliverables→Protocol→CSR register. The wiring is self-documenting on screen, not buried in code.

What must NOT cross the seam

The adapter carries operational metadata only. The fields above are counts, percentages, dates, and statuses — never the underlying clinical record.

No PHI. No participant-level data. No reported clinical numbers. Nothing keyed to a participant crosses into the dashboard — no USUBJID/SUBJID records, no lab values, no AEs, no PK results, no demographics. The EDC reader sends the aggregate open-query count and clean/SDV percentages, not the queries or the case-report-form contents behind them. Reported and regulated values are produced and signed off in validated tools; the dashboard only watches the operational shadow of that work.

Implementation steps

1. Implement ctmsStudies() to return one record per active study with id, phase, the seven dated milestones, enrolled/target, and sites. This is the skeleton everything else attaches to.
2. Implement edcMetrics(id), tlfTrackerCounts(id), and deliverablesRegister(id) as per-study read-only pulls returning only the aggregate fields in the table above.
3. Confirm qtrend is an 8-element series whose last element equals current openQ (qtrend[7] = openQ), so the open-query sparkline and the "Query backlog rising" signal read correctly.
4. Inside loadPortfolio(), loop the studies and call mapToModel(e, t, d) to merge each source payload into the canonical field names.
5. Swap the one line: STUDIES = await loadPortfolio(). Leave every panel untouched.
6. Remove the synthetic seed and the SIMULATED badge only once live data is verified end-to-end; until then, leave the badge visible so no one mistakes demo data for real status.

Sanity checks after wiring

  • The KPI strip reconciles to your sources: Open queries (portfolio) equals the sum of each study's EDC openQ; Active studies equals your live study count.
  • The TLF funnel never shows a later stage exceeding an earlier one — confirm finalized ≤ qcd ≤ programmed ≤ planned coming out of the tracker.
  • The Timeline dimension responds to real milestone status: any milestone past its date and not done should surface a red dot on the milestone track and fire the "Milestone overdue" signal.
  • daysToDBL derives from the live DBL milestone date, so the "DB lock at risk" signal and the Next-DB-lock KPI both move with CTMS.
Scope reminder. This monitor is the operational/program complement to TRIALMON_Dashboard.html: it watches deliverables, data readiness, TLF production, and milestones — not participant-level clinical signals. Participant-safety monitoring lives in TRIALMON. Keep that boundary when you wire sources: clinical signals belong there, operational metadata belongs here.

sections.innerHtml:
This dashboard (Study_Lifecycle_Monitor.html) is built around a small set of non-negotiable guardrails. They are not decoration: they decide what the tool is allowed to show, where its numbers may come from, who is allowed to act on them, and why it sits in its own lane rather than overlapping the participant-safety dashboard. Read this section before you put any output in front of a lead, a sponsor, or an inspector.

The five non-negotiables

  1. Operations-only. The dashboard watches deliverables, data readiness, TLF production, milestones, and programming throughput. It never ingests PHI, never holds participant-level rows (no USUBJID/SUBJID records), and never displays reported or regulated clinical values.
  2. Reported numbers come only from validated tools. Any number that lands in a CSR, a TLF, or a submission is produced and signed off in a validated environment (for example Phoenix WinNonlin for NCA/PK, Pinnacle 21 for conformance, the EDC/CTMS of record). This dashboard restates operational metadata about those deliverables — counts, percentages, dates — never the regulated results themselves.
  3. Transparent scoring — no black box. Every risk score is a published arithmetic formula with stated thresholds. The exact dimension formulas and the RAG cut-points are printed inside the dashboard itself (the collapsible note under the heatmap) and in the README. There is no predictive model and no learned weighting to audit.
  4. Humans decide. The tool ranks, flags, and routes attention. It does not approve a database lock, accept a query backlog, or sign off a TLF. Every signal is a prompt for a person to look, not an action.
  5. Simulated demo data, clearly labeled. The shipped portfolio is seeded synthetic data, badged SIMULATED DEMO DATA in the header, footer, and README, and reproducible from the fixed seed. None of it is real study data.

Not validated software. The Study Lifecycle Monitor is decision-support, not a validated system. Do not treat any figure on it as the source of truth for a regulated deliverable, and do not cite it in a submission. When an operational number matters, confirm it against the system of record (CTMS, EDC, the TLF program tracker, and the relevant validated tools) before acting.

What "operations-only" rules out

The boundary is concrete. The following never cross into this dashboard, even via the live data adapter:

  • PHI or any directly or indirectly identifying participant information.
  • Participant-level records of any kind (one row per participant, per visit, or per event).
  • Reported clinical numbers — efficacy estimates, PK parameters, safety incidences, anything that would appear in a TLF or CSR result.
  • Unblinded treatment information.

What does cross is read-only operational metadata: study phase, milestone dates and statuses, enrolled/target counts, site counts, open-query counts and their weekly trend, clean and SDV percentages, days to DB lock, TLF stage counts, deliverable done/total tallies, and double-programming percentage with open-discrepancy count. These are program-management facts about the conduct of the work, not clinical observations from it.

A simple test for whether a field belongs here: if removing every participant from the study would leave the number undefined, it does not belong (that is participant-level data). An open-query count belongs; the content of any query does not. A TLF finalized count belongs; the values inside the TLF do not.

Transparent scoring: the formulas are on the wall

Because there is no model to trust, the scoring must be legible. Each study carries four dimension scores plus a weighted overall, all clamped to [0,100]. The collapsible note under the heatmap quotes them; they are reproduced here so you can reconcile any cell by hand.

Timeline = clamp( overdue×22 + dblPressure(s)×45 )
overdue = count of milestones with status risk; dblPressure: Startup = .15; otherwise by days-to-DB-lock d: d≤0 → 1, d<14 → .85, d<30 → .6, d<60 → .35, else .2

Data = clamp( ( min(1, openQ/120)×45 + (1−cleanPct)×30 + (1−sdvPct)×25 ) × (0.5 + 0.6×dblPressure(s)) )

TLF = clamp( (1 − finalized/planned)×100 × (0.3 + 0.8×repPressure(s)) )
repPressure: Reporting = 1, DB Lock = .6, Conduct = .25, else .1

Resource = clamp( (1 − dblProgPct/100)×55 + min(1, discrep/18)×45×repPressure(s) )

Overall = clamp( 0.30×Timeline + 0.28×Data + 0.27×TLF + 0.15×Resource )

RAG thresholds — rag(v): green if v≤33, amber (watch) if 33<v≤66, red (at risk) if v>66. Equivalently rag returns r when v>66, a when v>33, else g. clamp bounds every score to [0,100].

Two consequences are worth internalizing as a user. First, the overall weighting leans on Timeline and Data (0.30 + 0.28 = 58% of the score), so a study can turn red on schedule and data pressure even with TLF and Resource quiet — that is intended for a program lens. Second, both the Data and TLF dimensions are amplified by phase pressure, so the same raw open-query count or finalized fraction scores higher as a study approaches DB lock or enters Reporting. When a score moves and the raw inputs did not, check whether the study changed phase or its days-to-DB-lock crossed a pressure band.

The deliberate two-dashboard split

This is a conscious separation of concerns, not a gap. There are two dashboards and they do not overlap:

DashboardWatchesDoes not watch
Study_Lifecycle_Monitor.html (this one)Deliverables, data readiness, TLF production, milestones, programming throughput — the operational/program lifecycleAnything participant-level or clinical
TRIALMON_Dashboard.html (companion)Participant-safety monitoringProgram/operational deliverables

The README states the relationship plainly: this Study Lifecycle Monitor is the operational/program complement to TRIALMON. If you are asking "is the work on track to be delivered, locked, and reported?" you are on the right dashboard. If you are asking "is a participant or the population showing a safety signal?" that question belongs to TRIALMON, and nothing here is designed or governed to answer it.

Do not use operational health as a proxy for participant safety. A study can be green across all four operational dimensions and still have a safety concern that only TRIALMON would surface, and the reverse holds as well. Keeping the two lenses separate is what keeps each one honest and within its governed data boundary.

Deliberately excluded

The README lists what was kept out by design, and the footer repeats the core of it. This is standard, defensible operational monitoring only:

  • No predictive model and no experimental engine of any kind.
  • No research-work-in-progress or manuscript-specific methods — none of the author's personal-research constructs are present anywhere in this tool.
  • No participant-level data, no PHI, no reported clinical numbers.

The footer states it for the record: self-contained, offline · SIMULATED DEMO DATA (seeded, reproducible) — not real study data. Operational monitoring only: no PHI, no participant-level data, no reported clinical numbers; reported values always come from validated tools. Transparent operational scoring — no predictive model and no experimental components.

Going live without breaking the guardrails

When the synthetic generator is replaced by real feeds, the governance posture must survive the swap. Follow this sequence.

1. Implement loadPortfolio() to return the exact same study-object shape the generator produces, sourced read-only from the systems of record (CTMS, EDC, the TLF program tracker, the Protocol→CSR deliverables register) via REST or a nightly CSV export.

2. Map only operational metadata into the model — the per-field source list in the data adapter documents exactly which system feeds each field. Confirm no PHI, no participant-level row, and no reported clinical value is in the payload.

3. Remove the SIMULATED DEMO DATA badging only once live data is confirmed wired and validated against the source systems; until then, leave every honesty label in place.

4. Keep the scoring formulas visible. If weights or thresholds are ever tuned, update the on-screen collapsible note and the README in the same change, so the displayed math always equals the executed math.

5. Re-verify after any change: the embedded JS should pass node --check, and a headless render should produce no NaN and a full green/amber/red spread, exactly as the demo build is verified.

Even live, the rule does not move: reported and regulated numbers stay with the validated tools and the systems of record (for example Phoenix WinNonlin, Pinnacle 21, the EDC/CTMS of record). The dashboard's job is to point a person at the right study and the right deliverable fast — the person, working in the validated system, makes the call.
